1. Skill Coverage Comprehensiveness (30.1a_covered: SQL_basics, SQL_advanced, Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, Seaborn, statistics, machine_learning
1. Skill Coverage Comprehensiveness (30.1b_comment: This is mostly a template/outline rather than a fully specified 30-day plan, so coverage can’t be credited beyond what’s explicitly present. SQL: only SELECT basics + JOINs are detailed; aggregation is mentioned at the week objective level, but there’s no explicit day content for GROUP BY/HAVING, subqueries/CTEs, window functions, set ops, or advanced patterns → very shallow. Python (Pandas/NumPy): week 2 is only stated as a goal with no concrete daily topics/tasks beyond 'Day 8: DataFrame basics…' → insufficient evidence of read/clean/aggregate/merge, time series, missing values, reshaping, vectorization, NumPy broadcasting, etc. Viz+Stats: week 3 objective mentions Matplotlib/Seaborn and descriptive stats/distributions/hypothesis testing, but no actual daily breakdown, practice tasks, or scope detail → partial at best. ML: mentions 'sklearn intro' and a project with 'simple modeling/prediction' but no explicit algorithms (e.g., linear/logistic regression, trees) specified → minimal credit.

2. Difficulty Progression Reasonableness.2a_structure_detection: 2/5 — has complete week division (9 weeks), detected 7 distinct Day(s)
2. Difficulty Progression Reasonableness.2b_comment:
1) Starting point (4/5): Beginning with SQL fundamentals is appropriate for an intermediate Python learner targeting analyst interviews; it avoids re-teaching Python basics and doesn’t jump to ML. Minor deduction because the plan doesn’t explicitly justify why SQL comes first or include a quick setup/onboarding day (DB environment, practice platform choice), which can be a real friction point.

2) Inter-week progression (5/5): Week-to-week ordering matches the recommended route (SQL -> Pandas/NumPy -> Viz/Stats -> ML -> projects). The progression is coherent and interview-aligned.

3) Intra-day / within-week progression (2/5): Only Day 1-2 are concretely specified; Days 3-7, 8-14, 15-21, 22+ are largely placeholders (“continued...”, “...”), so it’s not possible to verify a true daily ramp from basics to application. What is shown (SELECT basics -> JOINs) is reasonable, but the evidence is incomplete, so strict scoring requires a significant deduction.

4) Comprehensive project (4/5): End-of-plan projects are clearly included and integrate multiple skills (SQL extraction + Pandas + visualization + simple modeling). Deduct 1 point because project scope is likely too large for 2–3 hours/day across only Days 28–30 (especially including modeling), and success criteria/deliverables aren’t specified (e.g., notebook + cleaned dataset + brief report/dashboard + key interview-style insights).

3. Resource Authenticity & Quality (20 p.3b_comment:
Authenticity (4/4): The cited platforms and example links are real and well-known (freeCodeCamp YouTube SQL full course, W3Schools SQL SELECT page, SQLBolt, Mode SQL tutorial, Coursera/IBM, DataCamp, Kaggle Learn, LeetCode, HackerRank, Yahoo Finance). No obviously fabricated domains/pages are shown in the excerpt.
Specificity (2/4): A few items are nicely specific (e.g., freeCodeCamp “first 1 hour”, SQLBolt “Lesson 1-3” and “Lesson 6-7”, Mosh “15 min”, W3Schools “SELECT Statement”). However, many later parts are placeholders (“Day 3-7 continued…”, “Day 8…”, “Day 15…”, “Day 22…”) and several resources lack exact course/module names or direct links (e.g., “SQL Joins Explained - Programming with Mosh” missing URL; Coursera/DataCamp entries missing direct course links; Kaggle dataset referenced generically as “E-commerce Data”). That vagueness limits reproducibility.
Diversity (4/4): Strong mix of resource types: video (YouTube), reading/tutorial sites (W3Schools, Mode), interactive practice (SQLBolt), structured courses (Coursera, DataCamp, Kaggle Learn), coding challenge platforms (LeetCode/HackerRank), books, and project datasets (Kaggle/Yahoo Finance).

4. Practicality & Executability (15 pts).4b_comment:
Daily practice tasks (1/4): Only Day 1 and Day 2 show explicit, actionable practice with clear completion criteria (e.g., “finish SQLBolt Lessons 1–3/6–7” + a concrete query task). Most other days are placeholders (“Day 3–7 continued…”, “Day 8…”, “Day 15…”, “Day 22…”) with no specified exercises, so as written it does not provide a clear practice task every day; this is a major executability gap.

Time arrangement feasibility (1/3): The two filled days have reasonable 2.5h estimates aligned to a 2–3h/day schedule, but the majority of days have no time estimates or workload definition. Without per-day scope and timing, it’s not possible to verify that later topics (stats + visualization + ML + projects) fit consistently into 2–3 hours/day; risk of overload is high.

Comprehensive project quality (2/3): Projects use real-world sources (Kaggle e-commerce, Yahoo Finance) and propose integrating cleaning, visualization, and simple modeling. However, the SQL integration is not concretely executable (many Kaggle datasets are provided as CSV; “SQL extraction” is ambiguous unless you explicitly require loading into SQLite/Postgres and writing required queries). Deliverables (notebook/report, metrics, acceptance criteria) are not specified, reducing executability.

Overall: The plan is a strong outline, but in its current form it is not a fully executable 30-day schedule because most days lack explicit practice tasks and timeboxing.

5. Document Format & Extras (10 pts).5b_structure_completeness: 4/4 — User profile:Y 4-week division:Y(9 weeks) Appendix/Tips:Y Daily time:Y
